content.content:
# Top-level build/test tasks.
#
# `make test` rebuilds every fixture from clean installs and runs its test
# suite.
# 
PYTHON ?= python3.12

.PHONY: test test-service clean

test: test-service ## Build + test the fixture from clean

test-service: ## Python fixture (vendored CTFd 3.7.7): fresh venv, install, bounded pytest subset
	cd fixtures/service && \
		$(PYTHON) -m venv .venv && \
		.venv/bin/pip install --quiet --upgrade pip && \
		.venv/bin/pip install --quiet -r requirements.txt && \
		grep -vE "^-r |psycopg2|bandit|sphinx|pip-tools|flask_profiler|flask-debugtoolbar|pipdeptree" development.txt > .dev-test.txt && \
		.venv/bin/pip install --quiet -r .dev-test.txt && \
		.venv/bin/python -m pytest tests/test_config.py tests/users -q -p no:randomly

clean: ## Remove build artifacts and virtualenvs
	rm -rf fixtures/service/.venv
--- service Makefile ---
lint:
	ruff check --select E,F,W,B,C4,I --ignore E402,E501,E712,B904,B905,I001 --exclude=CTFd/uploads CTFd/ migrations/ tests/
	isort --profile=black --check-only --skip=CTFd/uploads --skip-glob **/node_modules CTFd/ tests/
	yarn --cwd CTFd/themes/admin lint
	black --check --diff --exclude=CTFd/uploads --exclude=node_modules .
	prettier --check 'CTFd/themes/*/assets/**/*'
	prettier --check '**/*.md'

format:
	isort --profile=black --skip=CTFd/uploads --skip-glob **/node_modules CTFd/ tests/
	black --exclude=CTFd/uploads --exclude=node_modules .
	prettier --write 'CTFd/themes/**/assets/**/*'
	prettier --write '**/*.md'

test:
	pytest -rf --cov=CTFd --cov-context=test --cov-report=xml \
		--ignore-glob="**/node_modules/" \
		--ignore=node_modules/ \
		-W ignore::sqlalchemy.exc.SADeprecationWarning \
		-W ignore::sqlalchemy.exc.SAWarning \
		-n auto
	bandit -r CTFd -x CTFd/uploads --skip B105,B322
	pipdeptree

coverage:
	coverage html --show-contexts

serve:
	python serve.py

shell:
	python manage.py shell

translations-init:
	# make translations-init lang=af
	pybabel init -i messages.pot -d CTFd/translations -l $(lang)

translations-extract:
	pybabel extract -F babel.cfg -k lazy_gettext -k _l -o messages.pot .

translations-update:
	pybabel update --ignore-obsolete -i messages.pot -d CTFd/translations

translations-compile:
	pybabel compile -f -d CTFd/translations

translations-lint:
	dennis-cmd lint CTFd/translations

#!/bin/bash

ROOTDIR=`pwd -P`
docker run \
    --rm \
    --entrypoint bash \
    -v $ROOTDIR:/mnt/CTFd \
    -e CUSTOM_COMPILE_COMMAND='./scripts/pip-compile.sh' \
    -it python:3.11-slim-bookworm \
    -c 'cd /mnt/CTFd && pip install pip-tools==7.4.1 && pip-compile'

input.prompt:
You are remediating known-vulnerable Python dependencies in a CTFd fixture repo (vendored CTFd 3.7.7) at:
/tmp/bomly-study-9x8vcaqn/claude-mcp-service-1-1783664138/repo/fixtures/service

This repo is used in a larger task; your job is ONLY the Python dependency remediation in this `service` directory. Do not touch anything under CTFd/themes/ (another agent handles npm deps there).

## Background

`requirements.in` is the source file of direct pins; `requirements.txt` is a pip-compile-generated fully-pinned lockfile (see header comment — normally generated via `./scripts/pip-compile.sh`, a Docker-based script using `pip-tools==7.4.1` on python:3.11-slim-bookworm). You have network access to PyPI. You have python3.12 available, which is what the repo's root Makefile actually uses (PYTHON ?= python3.12), so use python3.12 for your working venv(s) even though the lockfile header mentions 3.11 — matching the Makefile's actual test runner matters more than the stale header comment.

The root Makefile's `make test` target (the ONLY acceptance gate that matters — read it yourself at /tmp/bomly-study-9x8vcaqn/claude-mcp-service-1-1783664138/repo/Makefile) does, roughly:
```
cd fixtures/service
python3.12 -m venv .venv
.venv/bin/pip install --quiet --upgrade pip
.venv/bin/pip install --quiet -r requirements.txt
grep -vE "^-r |psycopg2|bandit|sphinx|pip-tools|flask_profiler|flask-debugtoolbar|pipdeptree" development.txt > .dev-test.txt
.venv/bin/pip install --quiet -r .dev-test.txt
.venv/bin/python -m pytest tests/test_config.py tests/users -q -p no:randomly
```
Your final deliverable MUST make `make test` pass when run from the repo root (`/tmp/bomly-study-9x8vcaqn/claude-mcp-service-1-1783664138/repo`). Iterate with this exact command (or the manual steps above) until it's green. Feel free to run it multiple times as you iterate.

## Vulnerable packages to remediate (from a bomly dependency scan)

Direct pins in requirements.in that must be bumped to at least these versions (these are the minimum versions that close ALL CVEs bomly found for that package — you may go higher, e.g. to the latest compatible release, if that's cleaner, but must be at least this high):

- Flask: 2.0.3 -> >=3.1.3 (CVE-2023-30861 fixed in 2.2.5; a second finding, CVE-2026-27205, needs >=3.1.3 — there is no fix within the Flask 2.x line for the second one, so this forces a major-version bump to Flask 3.x, which in turn forces Werkzeug 3.x)
- Werkzeug: 2.0.3 -> >=3.1.6 (multiple CVEs; the highest-versioned fix required is 3.1.6)
- cryptography: 40.0.2 -> >=48.0.1 (multiple CVEs incl. vulnerable vendored OpenSSL; this is a TRANSITIVE dep — not listed in requirements.in directly, pulled in via pymysql[rsa]. You likely need to add an explicit `cryptography>=48.0.1` line to requirements.in to force pip-compile to pick a safe version, since nothing else in the tree currently forces it that high)
- urllib3: 1.26.20 -> >=2.7.0 (multiple CVEs incl. decompression bomb bypass, cross-origin header leak). NOTE: urllib3 is also pulled in transitively by botocore (via boto3==1.35.27) and requests. Verify botocore's version constraint on urllib3 doesn't conflict — check botocore's actual constraint at the resolved version; if boto3/botocore needs bumping too to accept urllib3 2.x, bump boto3 to a compatible version (there is no known CVE on boto3/botocore itself per the scan, so only bump it if required for the urllib3 upgrade to resolve).
- mako: 1.1.3 -> >=1.3.12 (path traversal + ReDoS CVEs). TRANSITIVE dep, pulled in via alembic (used by flask-migrate and dataset). Not in requirements.in. Add an explicit `mako>=1.3.12` line to requirements.in if pip-compile doesn't naturally resolve to a safe version otherwise.
- pillow: 10.1.0 -> >=12.2.0 (critical CVE-2023-50447 arbitrary code execution, buffer overflow, PDF parsing DoS, font integer overflow — the highest-versioned fix required is 12.2.0)
- pymysql: 1.0.2 -> >=1.1.1 (CVE-2024-36039 SQL injection)
- idna: 2.10 -> >=3.15 (CVE-2024-3651 DoS + a bypass of that fix). TRANSITIVE dep pulled in via requests. Not in requirements.in; should resolve naturally to a safe version once you regenerate the lockfile (requests>=2.32 doesn't hard-pin idna<3), but verify and add an explicit `idna>=3.15` line to requirements.in if it doesn't.
- pydantic: 1.6.2 -> >=1.10.13 (CVE-2024-3772 ReDoS). IMPORTANT: stay within the pydantic 1.x line (do NOT jump to pydantic 2.x) — pydantic 2 has a breaking API change (e.g. different validator/Config API) and nothing in this scan requires the 2.x line; 1.10.13 is the last 1.x release and fixes the CVE.
- requests: 2.32.3 -> >=2.33.0 (CVE-2024-47081 .netrc leak, CVE-2026-25645 insecure temp file reuse)
- python-dotenv: 0.13.0 -> >=1.2.2 (CVE-2026-28684 symlink following arbitrary file overwrite in set_key)

## Process

1. Edit `requirements.in` bumping the direct pins listed above (Flask, Werkzeug, requests, PyMySQL[rsa] (keep the [rsa] extra), python-dotenv, pydantic (1.x only), Pillow, urllib3). For the transitive-only ones (cryptography, mako, idna), first try regenerating the lockfile without touching requirements.in (upgrading the direct deps above may pull them up on its own); only add explicit lines to requirements.in for whichever of cryptography/mako/idna still resolve below the required minimum.
2. Set up a venv, `pip install pip-tools==7.4.1`, and run pip-compile against requirements.in to regenerate requirements.txt (use `--upgrade` or targeted `-P <pkg>` upgrades as needed; preserve the existing header/comment style if pip-compile regenerates it automatically — that's fine and expected).
3. Bumping Flask to 3.x and Werkzeug to 3.x is a major-version jump that may break: (a) other Flask extensions pinned in requirements.in that don't support Flask 3 (flask-restx==1.1.0, flask-sqlalchemy==2.5.1, flask-migrate==2.5.3, flask-script==2.0.6, flask-caching==2.0.2, flask-babel==2.0.0, flask-marshmallow==0.10.1), and (b) CTFd's own application source code (under `CTFd/`) if it uses Flask/Werkzeug APIs removed or changed in Flask 3 (e.g. `before_first_request` removed in Flask 2.3+, changes to `flask.json`, blueprint/signal changes, `itsdangerous` serializer signature changes, click version bump affecting CLI commands, etc.).
   - For (a): bump only the specific Flask-extension packages that actually fail to resolve or fail at runtime, to the minimum version that supports Flask 3. Check each package's PyPI release notes/changelog if needed. Don't bump ones that already work fine.
   - For (b): if `make test` fails with a clear Flask/Werkzeug 3.x incompatibility in CTFd's own source, make the MINIMAL source code change needed to restore compatibility. Do not refactor unrelated code, do not add features. Only change what's required for correctness under the new dependency versions.
4. Iterate: regenerate lockfile / adjust versions / fix source / re-run `make test` from the repo root, until it passes.
5. Sanity-check the final requirements.txt: confirm none of the 11 target packages above are still pinned below their required minimum version.

## What NOT to do

- Do not touch anything under `fixtures/service/CTFd/themes/` (npm/yarn deps — handled separately).
- Do not upgrade packages that have no listed CVE unless strictly required to resolve a dependency conflict caused by the required upgrades above (if you do, note why in your report).
- Do not use `--no-verify`-style shortcuts, don't skip pytest, don't weaken test assertions to force a pass — if a test fails for a legitimate behavioral reason, fix the underlying code/config, don't fix the test.

## Report back (be precise, this feeds into a FIXES.md changelog)

For each of the 11 target packages: old version -> new version actually pinned in requirements.txt.
Any other packages you had to bump for compatibility, with old -> new version and why.
Any CTFd source files you modified for Flask/Werkzeug 3 compatibility, with a one-line reason each.
Confirmation that `make test` passes from the repo root (paste the final pytest summary line).
Any of the 11 packages you could NOT fully remediate to the target version, and why (e.g. genuine unresolvable conflict) — be honest, do not claim success if `make test` doesn't actually pass.

- mako: 1.1.3 -> >=1.3.12 (path traversal + ReDoS CVEs). TRANSITIVE dep, pulled in via alembic (used by flask-migrate and dataset). Not in requirements.in. Add an explicit `mako>=1.3.12` line to requirements.in if pip-compile doesn't naturally resolve to a safe version otherwise.
- pillow: 10.1.0 -> >=12.2.0 (critical CVE-2023-50447 arbitrary code execution, buffer overflow, PDF parsing DoS, font integer overflow — the highest-versioned fix required is 12.2.0)
- pymysql: 1.0.2 -> >=1.1.1 (CVE-2024-36039 SQL injection)
- idna: 2.10 -> >=3.15 (CVE-2024-3651 DoS + a bypass of that fix). TRANSITIVE dep pulled in via requests. Not in requirements.in; should resolve naturally to a safe version once you regenerate the lockfile (requests>=2.32 doesn't hard-pin idna<3), but verify and add an explicit `idna>=3.15` line to requirements.in if it doesn't.
- pydantic: 1.6.2 -> >=1.10.13 (CVE-2024-3772 ReDoS). IMPORTANT: stay within the pydantic 1.x line (do NOT jump to pydantic 2.x) — pydantic 2 has a breaking API change (e.g. different validator/Config API) and nothing in this scan requires the 2.x line; 1.10.13 is the last 1.x release and fixes the CVE.
- requests: 2.32.3 -> >=2.33.0 (CVE-2024-47081 .netrc leak, CVE-2026-25645 insecure temp file reuse)
- python-dotenv: 0.13.0 -> >=1.2.2 (CVE-2026-28684 symlink following arbitrary file overwrite in set_key)

## Process

1. Edit `requirements.in` bumping the direct pins listed above (Flask, Werkzeug, requests, PyMySQL[rsa] (keep the [rsa] extra), python-dotenv, pydantic (1.x only), Pillow, urllib3). For the transitive-only ones (cryptography, mako, idna), first try regenerating the lockfile without touching requirements.in (upgrading the direct deps above may pull them up on its own); only add explicit lines to requirements.in for whichever of cryptography/mako/idna still resolve below the required minimum.
2. Set up a venv, `pip install pip-tools==7.4.1`, and run pip-compile against requirements.in to regenerate requirements.txt (use `--upgrade` or targeted `-P <pkg>` upgrades as needed; preserve the existing header/comment style if pip-compile regenerates it automatically — that's fine and expected).
3. Bumping Flask to 3.x and Werkzeug to 3.x is a major-version jump that may break: (a) other Flask extensions pinned in requirements.in that don't support Flask 3 (flask-restx==1.1.0, flask-sqlalchemy==2.5.1, flask-migrate==2.5.3, flask-script==2.0.6, flask-caching==2.0.2, flask-babel==2.0.0, flask-marshmallow==0.10.1), and (b) CTFd's own application source code (under `CTFd/`) if it uses Flask/Werkzeug APIs removed or changed in Flask 3 (e.g. `before_first_request` removed in Flask 2.3+, changes to `flask.json`, blueprint/signal changes, `itsdangerous` serializer signature changes, click version bump affecting CLI commands, etc.).
   - For (a): bump only the specific Flask-extension packages that actually fail to resolve or fail at runtime, to the minimum version that supports Flask 3. Check each package's PyPI release notes/changelog if needed. Don't bump ones that already work fine.
   - For (b): if `make test` fails with a clear Flask/Werkzeug 3.x incompatibility in CTFd's own source, make the MINIMAL source code change needed to restore compatibility. Do not refactor unrelated code, do not add features. Only change what's required for correctness under the new dependency versions.
4. Iterate: regenerate lockfile / adjust versions / fix source / re-run `make test` from the repo root, until it passes.
5. Sanity-check the final requirements.txt: confirm none of the 11 target packages above are still pinned below their required minimum version.

## What NOT to do

- Do not touch anything under `fixtures/service/CTFd/themes/` (npm/yarn deps — handled separately).
- Do not upgrade packages that have no listed CVE unless strictly required to resolve a dependency conflict caused by the required upgrades above (if you do, note why in your report).
- Do not use `--no-verify`-style shortcuts, don't skip pytest, don't weaken test assertions to force a pass — if a test fails for a legitimate behavioral reason, fix the underlying code/config, don't fix the test.

## Report back (be precise, this feeds into a FIXES.md changelog)

For each of the 11 target packages: old version -> new version actually pinned in requirements.txt.
Any other packages you had to bump for compatibility, with old -> new version and why.
Any CTFd source files you modified for Flask/Werkzeug 3 compatibility, with a one-line reason each.
Confirmation that `make test` passes from the repo root (paste the final pytest summary line).
Any of the 11 packages you could NOT fully remediate to the target version, and why (e.g. genuine unresolvable conflict) — be honest, do not claim success if `make test` doesn't actually pass.

# Top-level build/test tasks.
#
# `make test` rebuilds every fixture from clean installs and runs its test
# suite.
# 
PYTHON ?= python3.12

.PHONY: test test-service clean

test: test-service ## Build + test the fixture from clean

test-service: ## Python fixture (vendored CTFd 3.7.7): fresh venv, install, bounded pytest subset
	cd fixtures/service && \
		$(PYTHON) -m venv .venv && \
		.venv/bin/pip install --quiet --upgrade pip && \
		.venv/bin/pip install --quiet -r requirements.txt && \
		grep -vE "^-r |psycopg2|bandit|sphinx|pip-tools|flask_profiler|flask-debugtoolbar|pipdeptree" development.txt > .dev-test.txt && \
		.venv/bin/pip install --quiet -r .dev-test.txt && \
		.venv/bin/python -m pytest tests/test_config.py tests/users -q -p no:randomly

clean: ## Remove build artifacts and virtualenvs
	rm -rf fixtures/service/.venv

input.prompt:
You are remediating known-vulnerable npm/yarn dependencies in two CTFd frontend theme packages (part of a larger fixture repo remediation task). The two theme directories are independent yarn projects:

- /tmp/bomly-study-9x8vcaqn/claude-mcp-service-1-1783664138/repo/fixtures/service/CTFd/themes/admin
- /tmp/bomly-study-9x8vcaqn/claude-mcp-service-1-1783664138/repo/fixtures/service/CTFd/themes/core-beta

Each has its own package.json and yarn.lock. Only touch these two directories. Do not touch fixtures/service/requirements*.txt or anything Python-related (another agent handles that).

## Environment

Node v22 is available. `yarn` is NOT installed globally — enable it via corepack (`corepack enable` then `corepack prepare yarn@1.22.22 --activate`, or `npm install -g yarn`) since these projects use yarn.lock (classic yarn v1 lockfile format — check the yarn.lock header/format to confirm which yarn major version to use; these look like yarn v1 "classic" lockfiles based on their structure). There is network access to the npm registry.

## Important: one vulnerability has NO fix — do not attempt to "fix" it by bumping

`@vitejs/plugin-vue` (version 5.1.4 in admin, version 3.0.1 in core-beta) has an advisory GHSA-hxxj-55p6-h93r ("Malware in plugin-vue"), severity critical, with `fix_state: not-fixed` and `affected_version_range: >=0` (i.e. it applies to every published version of the package — there is no clean version to move to). Bomly's own remediation output confirms: `"action":"no-fix-upstream", "recommendation":"No fixed version released for GHSA-hxxj-55p6-h93r. Remove or replace the dependency, or acknowledge via allow_vulnerability_ids."`

Do NOT bump @vitejs/plugin-vue to any other version claiming it fixes this — no version fixes it. Since @vitejs/plugin-vue is required for Vite to build Vue single-file components (a core part of both themes' build), removing it would break the build / remove functionality, which is out of scope for a dependency-version remediation task. Leave @vitejs/plugin-vue's version alone (just make sure whatever version ends up in yarn.lock after your other bumps is still a normal, non-malicious release — i.e. don't let it accidentally get bumped to something weird during your yarn install churn). You will report this one as "no fix available" — that's the correct, honest outcome, not a failure on your part.

## Other vulnerable packages to remediate (from a bomly scan of each theme)

### admin theme (fixtures/service/CTFd/themes/admin)

Direct devDependency bumps in package.json:
- `vite`: currently ^5.1.2 -> bump to a version satisfying >=6.4.3 (closes several CVEs: launch-editor command injection, server.fs.deny bypasses, path traversal in optimized deps .map handling — 6.4.3 is the highest floor required across all the individual findings)
- `eslint`: currently ^8.56.0 — no CVE on eslint itself, but its transitive deps are vulnerable (minimatch, flatted, cross-spawn, ajv, brace-expansion, js-yaml). Prefer fixing via yarn `resolutions` in package.json rather than a major eslint bump (eslint 9 requires flat config migration, out of scope): add resolutions for minimatch>=3.1.4, flatted>=3.4.2, cross-spawn>=7.0.5, ajv>=6.14.0, brace-expansion>=1.1.13, js-yaml>=4.2.0 (only include the ones actually needed after checking what versions resolve).
- `markdown-it`: currently ^14.0.0 -> bump to >=14.2.0 (fixes 2 ReDoS CVEs). Its transitive dep `linkify-it` also needs >=5.0.1 — check if the markdown-it bump pulls that automatically; add a `resolutions` entry if not.
- `echarts`: currently ^5.4.3 -> bump to >=6.1.0 (XSS CVE GHSA-fgmj-fm8m-jvvx, only fixed in the 6.x line). This is a MAJOR version bump of a runtime (non-dev) dependency actually used in the theme's JS/Vue code (search `assets/` for `echarts` usage) — after bumping, run the build (`yarn build` i.e. `vite build`) and grep the echarts usage sites against the v6 migration guide / changelog for breaking API changes (module registration via `use()`, import paths, etc. changed between echarts 5 and 6). Fix any build errors or obviously-broken import paths minimally. If usage is minimal (e.g. just a basic chart render call) this is likely low-risk, but verify by actually building.
- `sass`: currently ^1.70.0 — vulnerable transitive deps via chokidar (picomatch, braces, immutable). Add `resolutions`: picomatch>=2.3.2, braces>=3.0.3, immutable>=4.3.8.
- `rollup-plugin-copy`: currently ^3.5.0 — vulnerable transitive deps (@types/minimatch, micromatch via globby). Add `resolutions`: @types/minimatch>=5.1.8, micromatch>=4.0.8 (check exact versions needed after resolving).
- `@vue/compiler-sfc`: currently ^3.3.13 — vulnerable transitive postcss/nanoid (pulled in regardless via vite/vue too). Add `resolutions`: postcss>=8.5.10, nanoid>=3.3.8.
- Also add a `resolutions` entry for `rollup>=4.59.0` and `esbuild>=0.25.0` (both pulled in transitively by vite and vulnerable at the currently-resolved versions) if bumping vite itself to >=6.4.3 doesn't already bring these up to safe versions on its own — check after your vite bump before adding redundant resolutions.

### core-beta theme (fixtures/service/CTFd/themes/core-beta)

Direct dependency/devDependency bumps in package.json:
- `vite`: currently ^3.0.5 -> bump to a version satisfying >=6.4.3 (same reasoning as admin; this is a bigger jump since core-beta is still on Vite 3). Check core-beta's `vite.config.js`/build scripts for Vite 3-specific config that may need minor updates for Vite 6 (e.g. plugin API changes, `build.rollupOptions` behavior) — verify by running `yarn build`.
- `echarts`: currently ^5.3.2 -> bump to >=6.1.0 (same XSS CVE as admin). Same verification approach: check `assets/` usage, build, fix minimal breakage.
- `sass`: currently ^1.49.7 — same transitive picomatch/braces/immutable issue as admin, same resolutions.
- `rollup-plugin-copy`: currently ^3.4.0 — same transitive @types/minimatch/minimatch/micromatch issue, add resolutions.
- `vue`: currently ^3.2.25 — vulnerable transitive postcss/nanoid via @vue/compiler-sfc pulled in by vue itself. Add resolutions: postcss>=8.5.10, nanoid>=3.3.8.
- Add `resolutions` for `rollup>=2.80.0` and `esbuild>=0.25.0` if the vite bump doesn't already resolve these (core-beta's vite 3 pulls rollup 2.x, not rollup 4.x like admin — check what the vite 6 bump actually resolves to for rollup before deciding).
- core-beta does NOT have eslint or markdown-it as dependencies — skip those, only relevant to admin.

## Process for each theme directory

1. `cd` into the theme directory.
2. Edit `package.json`: bump the direct dependency versions listed above, and add a `resolutions` block (yarn-specific field) for the transitive overrides listed above — only include entries you've confirmed are actually needed (i.e. the currently-resolved transitive version is below the safe floor).
3. Run `yarn install` (delete yarn.lock first is NOT necessary/desired — yarn will update it in place; do NOT delete node_modules/yarn.lock wholesale unless install is stuck, prefer incremental resolution).
4. Run `yarn build` (or `npx vite build` if no build script) to confirm the theme still builds successfully. There is no automated test suite for these themes — a successful build is your correctness signal. If the build fails, diagnose whether it's a genuine breaking API change from one of your version bumps (fix minimally in the relevant `assets/` source file) vs. a config issue (fix the vite config minimally).
5. After a successful build, re-check with a fresh look at yarn.lock (or ask me to re-scan) that the target packages (vite, echarts, markdown-it, sass's transitive picomatch/braces/immutable, rollup-plugin-copy's transitive minimatch chain, @vue/compiler-sfc or vue's transitive postcss/nanoid, eslint's transitive chain for admin) are now at or above the safe floors listed above. You can grep yarn.lock for `"<pkg>@` entries and check resolved versions.

## What NOT to do

- Don't touch @vitejs/plugin-vue's version (see above — no fix exists, leave it, just report it as unfixable).
- Don't do a major eslint version bump (8->9) — use resolutions instead to patch its transitive deps.
- Don't touch Python files or fixtures/service/requirements*.
- Don't add features or refactor unrelated theme code — only what's needed to build successfully after the dependency bumps.

## Report back (precise — feeds into a FIXES.md changelog)

For EACH theme separately (admin, core-beta):
- Each target package: old version -> new version actually resolved in yarn.lock (for direct bumps) or old transitive version -> new transitive version (for resolutions overrides).
- Confirm `yarn build` succeeded (paste the final success/output line) — or explain exactly what failed and what you tried.
- Any theme source files (under assets/) you modified for echarts v6 (or other) API compatibility, with a one-line reason each.
- Explicitly state that @vitejs/plugin-vue (GHSA-hxxj-55p6-h93r) has no available fix and was left unchanged.
- Any package you could not fully remediate to the target floor, and why — be honest, don't claim success if the build doesn't actually pass.

- `echarts`: currently ^5.4.3 -> bump to >=6.1.0 (XSS CVE GHSA-fgmj-fm8m-jvvx, only fixed in the 6.x line). This is a MAJOR version bump of a runtime (non-dev) dependency actually used in the theme's JS/Vue code (search `assets/` for `echarts` usage) — after bumping, run the build (`yarn build` i.e. `vite build`) and grep the echarts usage sites against the v6 migration guide / changelog for breaking API changes (module registration via `use()`, import paths, etc. changed between echarts 5 and 6). Fix any build errors or obviously-broken import paths minimally. If usage is minimal (e.g. just a basic chart render call) this is likely low-risk, but verify by actually building.
- `sass`: currently ^1.70.0 — vulnerable transitive deps via chokidar (picomatch, braces, immutable). Add `resolutions`: picomatch>=2.3.2, braces>=3.0.3, immutable>=4.3.8.
- `rollup-plugin-copy`: currently ^3.5.0 — vulnerable transitive deps (@types/minimatch, micromatch via globby). Add `resolutions`: @types/minimatch>=5.1.8, micromatch>=4.0.8 (check exact versions needed after resolving).
- `@vue/compiler-sfc`: currently ^3.3.13 — vulnerable transitive postcss/nanoid (pulled in regardless via vite/vue too). Add `resolutions`: postcss>=8.5.10, nanoid>=3.3.8.
- Also add a `resolutions` entry for `rollup>=4.59.0` and `esbuild>=0.25.0` (both pulled in transitively by vite and vulnerable at the currently-resolved versions) if bumping vite itself to >=6.4.3 doesn't already bring these up to safe versions on its own — check after your vite bump before adding redundant resolutions.

### core-beta theme (fixtures/service/CTFd/themes/core-beta)

Direct dependency/devDependency bumps in package.json:
- `vite`: currently ^3.0.5 -> bump to a version satisfying >=6.4.3 (same reasoning as admin; this is a bigger jump since core-beta is still on Vite 3). Check core-beta's `vite.config.js`/build scripts for Vite 3-specific config that may need minor updates for Vite 6 (e.g. plugin API changes, `build.rollupOptions` behavior) — verify by running `yarn build`.
- `echarts`: currently ^5.3.2 -> bump to >=6.1.0 (same XSS CVE as admin). Same verification approach: check `assets/` usage, build, fix minimal breakage.
- `sass`: currently ^1.49.7 — same transitive picomatch/braces/immutable issue as admin, same resolutions.
- `rollup-plugin-copy`: currently ^3.4.0 — same transitive @types/minimatch/minimatch/micromatch issue, add resolutions.
- `vue`: currently ^3.2.25 — vulnerable transitive postcss/nanoid via @vue/compiler-sfc pulled in by vue itself. Add resolutions: postcss>=8.5.10, nanoid>=3.3.8.
- Add `resolutions` for `rollup>=2.80.0` and `esbuild>=0.25.0` if the vite bump doesn't already resolve these (core-beta's vite 3 pulls rollup 2.x, not rollup 4.x like admin — check what the vite 6 bump actually resolves to for rollup before deciding).
- core-beta does NOT have eslint or markdown-it as dependencies — skip those, only relevant to admin.

## Process for each theme directory

1. `cd` into the theme directory.
2. Edit `package.json`: bump the direct dependency versions listed above, and add a `resolutions` block (yarn-specific field) for the transitive overrides listed above — only include entries you've confirmed are actually needed (i.e. the currently-resolved transitive version is below the safe floor).
3. Run `yarn install` (delete yarn.lock first is NOT necessary/desired — yarn will update it in place; do NOT delete node_modules/yarn.lock wholesale unless install is stuck, prefer incremental resolution).
4. Run `yarn build` (or `npx vite build` if no build script) to confirm the theme still builds successfully. There is no automated test suite for these themes — a successful build is your correctness signal. If the build fails, diagnose whether it's a genuine breaking API change from one of your version bumps (fix minimally in the relevant `assets/` source file) vs. a config issue (fix the vite config minimally).
5. After a successful build, re-check with a fresh look at yarn.lock (or ask me to re-scan) that the target packages (vite, echarts, markdown-it, sass's transitive picomatch/braces/immutable, rollup-plugin-copy's transitive minimatch chain, @vue/compiler-sfc or vue's transitive postcss/nanoid, eslint's transitive chain for admin) are now at or above the safe floors listed above. You can grep yarn.lock for `"<pkg>@` entries and check resolved versions.

## What NOT to do

- Don't touch @vitejs/plugin-vue's version (see above — no fix exists, leave it, just report it as unfixable).
- Don't do a major eslint version bump (8->9) — use resolutions instead to patch its transitive deps.
- Don't touch Python files or fixtures/service/requirements*.
- Don't add features or refactor unrelated theme code — only what's needed to build successfully after the dependency bumps.

## Report back (precise — feeds into a FIXES.md changelog)

For EACH theme separately (admin, core-beta):
- Each target package: old version -> new version actually resolved in yarn.lock (for direct bumps) or old transitive version -> new transitive version (for resolutions overrides).
- Confirm `yarn build` succeeded (paste the final success/output line) — or explain exactly what failed and what you tried.
- Any theme source files (under assets/) you modified for echarts v6 (or other) API compatibility, with a one-line reason each.
- Explicitly state that @vitejs/plugin-vue (GHSA-hxxj-55p6-h93r) has no available fix and was left unchanged.
- Any package you could not fully remediate to the target floor, and why — be honest, don't claim success if the build doesn't actually pass.

Saved ./pbm_check/pybluemonday-0.0.14-cp312-cp312-manylinux_2_17_aarch64.manylinux2014_aarch64.whl Successfully downloaded pybluemonday [notice] A new release of pip is available: 24.0 -> 26.1.2 [notice] To update, run: python3.12 -m pip install --upgrade pip pybluemonday-0.0.14-cp312-cp312-manylinux_2_17_aarch64.manylinux2014_aarch64.whl Metadata-Version: 2.1 Name: pybluemonday Version: 0.0.14 Summary: Python bindings for the bluemonday HTML sanitizer Home-page: https://github.com/ColdHeat/pybluemonday Author: Kevin Chung Author-email: kchung@nyu.edu Classifier: Programming Language :: Python :: 3 Classifier: License :: OSI Approved :: MIT License Classifier: Operating System :: OS Independent Requires-Python: >=3.7 Description-Content-Type: text/markdown License-File: LICENSE Requires-Dist: cffi~=1.1 # pybluemonday [![PyPI](https://img.shields.io/pypi/v/pybluemonday)](https://pypi.org/project/pybluemonday/) pybluemonday is a library for sanitizing HTML very quickly via [bluemonday](https://github.com/microcosm-cc/bluemonday). pybluemonday takes untrusted user generated content as an input, and will return HTML that has been sanitised against a whitelist of approved HTML elements and attributes so that you can safely include the content in your web page. **Note**: This library is in a useable state but is still experimental. It may not have feature parity with the actual bluemonday and is likely to sanitize HTML slightly differently than other libraries. PRs and feedback are welcome on improving this library. ## Installation ``` pip install pybluemonday ``` ## Examples ```python from pybluemonday import UGCPolicy s = UGCPolicy() print(s.sanitize("testing")) # testing s.AllowAttrs("class", "style").Globally() print(s.sanitize("testing")) # testing ``` ```python from pybluemonday import StrictPolicy s = StrictPolicy() s.sanitize("
Blog Post Title
") # Blog Post Title ``` ## How does this work? pybluemonday is a binding to [bluemonday](https://github.com/microcosm-cc/bluemonday) through a shared library built through cgo. However, instead of replicating the entire API, pybluemonday uses reflection on the Go side and some type checking on the Python side to call the right bluemonday function when you try to call a method. Essentially you want to create a Policy with the provided `pybluemonday.UGCPolicy`, `pybluemonday.StrictPolicy`, and `pybluemonday.NewPolicy` classes and then call methods that map to the appropriate [bluemonday struct method](https://pkg.go.dev/github.com/microcosm-cc/bluemonday#Policy). This is an open area of improvement but gets reasonable coverage of the original bluemonday interface. Also because it's difficult to share Go structs over to Python, pybluemonday keeps an ID reference to the struct in the Go side and passes the reference for every Go call. This means that if you corrupt or change the ID for some nonsensical reason you may likely end up with a memory leak. This is also an open area of improvement. ## Performance Most Python based HTML sanitizing libraries will need to rely on [html5lib](https://html5lib.readthedocs.io/en/latest/) for parsing HTML in a reasoanble way. Because of this you will likely see performance hits when using these libraries. Since pybluemonday is just bindings for [bluemonday](https://github.com/microcosm-cc/bluemonday) it has *very* good performance because all parsing and processing is done in Go by bluemonday. Go also ships an [HTML5 parser](https://godoc.org/golang.org/x/net/html) which means we avoid html5lib but still process HTML pretty well. Always take benchmarks with a grain of salt but when compared to other similar Python sanitizing libraries pybluemonday executes far faster: ![](benchmarks.png) ``` ❯ python benchmarks.py bleach (20000 sanitizations): 37.613802053 html_sanitizer (20000 sanitizations): 17.645683948 lxml Cleaner (20000 sanitizations): 10.500760227999997 pybluemonday (20000 sanitizations): 0.6188559669999876 ``` Benchmarks taken on a MacBook Pro 15-inch, 2016 (2.7 GHz Intel Core i7, 16 GB RAM)

for v in 40.0.2 41.0.7 42.0.8 43.0.3 44.0.3 45.0.7 46.0.0 46.0.7 47.0.0 48.0.0 48.0.1; do

  f=$(ls /tmp/cx_$v/*.whl 2>/dev/null | head -1)
  if [ -n "$f" ]; then

    python3 -c "
import zipfile
z = zipfile.ZipFile('$f')
for n in z.namelist():

from enum import IntEnum, unique
from typing import List
from unicodedata import normalize

from pybluemonday.bluemonday import ffi, lib

__version__ = "0.0.14"


@unique
class SandboxValue(IntEnum):
    SandboxAllowDownloads = 0
    SandboxAllowDownloadsWithoutUserActivation = 1
    SandboxAllowForms = 2
    SandboxAllowModals = 3
    SandboxAllowOrientationLock = 4
    SandboxAllowPointerLock = 5
    SandboxAllowPopups = 6
    SandboxAllowPopupsToEscapeSandbox = 7
    SandboxAllowPresentation = 8
    SandboxAllowSameOrigin = 9
    SandboxAllowScripts = 10
    SandboxAllowStorageAccessByUserActivation = 11
    SandboxAllowTopNavigation = 12
    SandboxAllowTopNavigationByUserActivation = 13


class AttrPolicyBuilder:
    def __init__(self, policy_id: int, policy_method: str, attrs: List[str]):
        self.policy_id = policy_id

content.content:
import os
import platform
import setuptools
import subprocess
import re

# Print out our uname for debugging purposes
uname = platform.uname()
print(uname)

# Install OSX Golang if needed
if uname.system == "Darwin":
    os.system("./scripts/setup-macos.sh")

# Install Linux Golang if needed
elif uname.system == "Linux":
    if uname.machine == "aarch64":
        os.system("./scripts/setup-arm64.sh")
    elif uname.machine in ("armv7l", "armv6l"):
        os.system("./scripts/setup-arm6vl.sh")
    elif uname.machine == "x86_64":
        os.system("./scripts/setup-linux-64.sh")
    elif uname.machine == "i686":
        os.system("./scripts/setup-linux-32.sh")

# Add in our downloaded Go compiler to PATH
old_path = os.environ["PATH"]
new_path = os.path.join(os.getcwd(), "go", "bin")
env = {"PATH": f"{old_path}:{new_path}"}
env = dict(os.environ, **env)
os.environ["PATH"] = f"{old_path}:{new_path}"

# Clean out any existing files
subprocess.call(["make", "clean"], env=env)

# Build the Go shared module for whatever OS we're on
subprocess.call(["make", "so"], env=env)

# Build the CFFI headers
subprocess.call(["pip", "install", "cffi~=1.1"], env=env)
subprocess.call(["make", "ffi"], env=env)

with open("pybluemonday/__init__.py", "r", encoding="utf8") as f:
    version = re.search(r'__version__ = "(.*?)"', f.read()).group(1)

with open("README.md", "r", encoding="utf8") as fh:
    long_description = fh.read()

setuptools.setup(
    name="pybluemonday",
    version=version,
    author="Kevin Chung",
    author_email="kchung@nyu.edu",
    description="Python bindings for the bluemonday HTML sanitizer",
    long_description=long_description,
    long_description_content_type="text/markdown",
    url="https://github.com/ColdHeat/pybluemonday",
    packages=setuptools.find_packages(),
    classifiers=[
        "Programming Language :: Python :: 3",

# cryptography is a transitive dep of PyMySQL[rsa]. It is capped at 46.0.0
# because pybluemonday==0.0.14 (the only version ever published; upstream
# repo unmaintained since 2024) hard-pins cffi~=1.1, while cryptography
# 46.0.1+ requires cffi>=2.0.0 on Python>=3.9. cryptography==46.0.0 is the
# highest release with a cffi>=1.14 constraint on this interpreter, so it's
# the ceiling pip's resolver will accept alongside pybluemonday.
PyMySQL[rsa]>=1.1.1

                if line.startswith('Requires-Dist'):

# -*- coding: utf-8 -*-
"""
    flask_marshmallow
    ~~~~~~~~~~~~~~~~~

    Integrates the marshmallow serialization/deserialization library
    with your Flask application.
"""
import warnings
from distutils.version import LooseVersion

from marshmallow import fields as base_fields, exceptions, pprint

from . import fields
from .schema import Schema

has_sqla = False
try:
    import flask_sqlalchemy  # noqa: F401
except ImportError:
    has_sqla = False
else:
    try:
        from . import sqla
    except ImportError:
        warnings.warn(
            "Flask-SQLAlchemy integration requires "
            "marshmallow-sqlalchemy to be installed."
        )
    else:
        has_sqla = True

__version__ = "0.14.0"
__version_info__ = tuple(LooseVersion(__version__).version)
__all__ = ["EXTENSION_NAME", "Marshmallow", "Schema", "fields", "exceptions", "pprint"]

import datetime
from collections import defaultdict

from flask_marshmallow import Marshmallow
from flask_sqlalchemy import SQLAlchemy
from sqlalchemy.ext.compiler import compiles
from sqlalchemy.ext.hybrid import hybrid_property
from sqlalchemy.orm import column_property, validates

from CTFd.cache import cache

db = SQLAlchemy()
ma = Marshmallow()


def get_class_by_tablename(tablename):
    """Return class reference mapped to table.
    https://stackoverflow.com/a/66666783

    :param tablename: String with name of table.
    :return: Class reference or None.
    """
    classes = []
    for m in db.Model.registry.mappers:
        c = m.class_
        if hasattr(c, "__tablename__") and c.__tablename__ == tablename:
            classes.append(c)

    # We didn't find this class
    if len(classes) == 0:
        return None
    # This is a class where we have only one possible candidate.
    # It's either a top level class or a polymorphic class with a specific hardcoded table name
    elif len(classes) == 1:
        return classes[0]
    # In this case we are dealing with a polymorphic table where all of the tables have the same table name.
    # However for us to identify the parent class we can look for the class that defines the polymorphic_on arg
    else:
        for c in classes:
            mapper_args = dict(c.__mapper_args__)
            if mapper_args.get("polymorphic_on") is not None:
                return c


@compiles(db.DateTime, "mysql")
def compile_datetime_mysql(_type, _compiler, **kw):
    """
    This decorator makes the default db.DateTime class always enable fsp to enable millisecond precision
    https://dev.mysql.com/doc/refman/5.7/en/fractional-seconds.html
    https://docs.sqlalchemy.org/en/14/core/custom_types.html#overriding-type-compilation
    """
    return "DATETIME(6)"


class Notifications(db.Model):
    __tablename__ = "notifications"
    id = db.Column(db.Integer, primary_key=True)
    title = db.Column(db.Text)
    content = db.Column(db.Text)
    date = db.Column(db.DateTime, default=datetime.datetime.utcnow)
